questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What ordering does a standard queue enforce? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Enqueue at the back, dequeue from the front—classic FIFO.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Core queue operations are typically named:? Choose the strongest option.
options: push / pop only (no other names exist)
enqueue / dequeue (offer/poll in some APIs), plus peek
questions.explanation: Enqueue inserts rear; dequeue removes front; peek reads front.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Dequeuing from an empty queue should:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Return a magic zero silently in all designs
Signal underflow / empty (exception or empty Optional)
questions.explanation: Empty dequeue is exceptional or explicitly empty—don’t invent data.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A circular buffer (ring) implements a bounded queue by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Shifting all elements on every dequeue
Allocating a new array on every enqueue
Using head/tail indices modulo capacity to reuse the array slot space
questions.explanation: Modulo arithmetic wraps indices so the fixed array is reused without shifting.

questions.stem: Why prefer a circular array over a naive “shift left on dequeue” array queue?
options: Avoid O(n) shifts; ring ops are O(1) time per enqueue/dequeue
questions.explanation: Shifting makes dequeue O(n); head/tail indices keep both ends O(1).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A deque (double-ended queue) allows:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Insert/remove at both front and back in O(1) (typical implementations)
questions.explanation: Deques support both ends; they can implement stacks or queues.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: In BFS on a graph, the queue’s role is to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Process nodes in LIFO order for depth-first discovery
Expand nodes level by level so first discovered is first expanded
questions.explanation: FIFO yields non-decreasing distance order in unweighted graphs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Implementing a queue with a singly linked list is efficient when you:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Enqueue and dequeue both at the head only
Scan for the minimum each time
Store nodes in a hash set only
Keep head and tail pointers: enqueue at tail, dequeue at head
questions.explanation: Two ends need two references; otherwise one operation becomes O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which real-world metaphor best matches a queue? Which response is most accurate?
options: People waiting in a single-file ticket line
A call stack of nested functions
questions.explanation: The first person in line is served first—FIFO.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Peek on a queue returns:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The front element (next to be dequeued) without removing it
questions.explanation: Peek/front observes without dequeue.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Bounded queue that is full on enqueue should typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Silently drop the front and the new item
Reject / block / apply backpressure per the chosen policy
Become unbounded automatically in all systems
questions.explanation: Full bounded queues need an explicit policy: error, block, or drop with rules.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Priority queues differ from FIFO queues because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Removal order follows priority, not arrival time alone
questions.explanation: Heaps/priority queues extract by priority; FIFO ignores keys.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: In Java, ArrayDeque as a queue is often preferred over LinkedList because:? Which answer is best?
options: Better locality and fewer per-node allocations for typical FIFO workloads
questions.explanation: ArrayDeque’s circular array usually outperforms pointer-heavy LinkedList for queues.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Space for n queued elements is:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: All pending elements must be stored—linear space.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which problem naturally uses a queue? Which response is most accurate?
options: Next greater element via monotonic stack only
questions.explanation: Level-order is BFS: enqueue children, dequeue parents.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A double-ended queue used as a stack means you:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Must enqueue only in the middle
Push and pop from the same end
questions.explanation: Stack discipline is LIFO at one end; deques support that easily.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Producer–consumer pipelines commonly place between stages:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A call stack shared across threads without locks
A singly linked list without any sync
questions.explanation: Queues decouple producers and consumers; bounds implement backpressure.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: In a ring buffer of capacity C, distinguishing full vs empty often needs:? Which answer is best?
options: A count, or an empty slot convention, or mirroring index tricks
questions.explanation: When head==tail, is it empty or full? Extra state or wasted slot resolves ambiguity.

questions.stem: Amortized complexity of enqueue on a resizable array queue (not shifting) is:
questions.explanation: Like dynamic arrays, occasional resize amortizes to O(1) per insert.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which statement is true? Which response is most accurate?
options: Queues cannot be implemented with linked lists
Queues are always slower than stacks by Big-O
Stacks and queues differ in which end removals use relative to insertions
questions.explanation: Both are restricted lists; FIFO vs LIFO is the semantic difference.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Implementing a queue with two stacks works by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Incoming stack for enqueue; outgoing stack for dequeue (pour when outgoing empty)
Pushing to both stacks on every enqueue
questions.explanation: Pour reverses twice → FIFO; amortized O(1) with lazy transfer.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Sliding window maximum in O(n) often uses a deque to store:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A full sorted copy of the array
All window indices in increasing order of value candidates (monotonic deque)
Only the window size as an integer
questions.explanation: Monotonic deque drops smaller useless indices from the back; front is max.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: 0-1 BFS (edges weight 0 or 1) uses a deque to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always push to back like Dijkstra with a binary heap only
Push weight-0 neighbors to front and weight-1 to back
questions.explanation: Deque maintains increasing distance order for 0-1 weights in linear time.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Design a circular queue (fixed capacity) API: enQueue when full should:? Which answer is best?
options: Resize by 2× silently in LeetCode-style fixed circular queue
Convert to a linked list mid-call
questions.explanation: Classic bounded circular queue problems reject when full.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Moving average from a data stream uses a queue to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Keep the last W elements, dequeue when window exceeds W, maintain a running sum
Store only the average without elements
questions.explanation: FIFO window drops oldest; sum updates in O(1).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Shortest path in an unweighted maze is computed with:? Choose the strongest option.
options: DFS that always finds shortest paths
questions.explanation: Unweighted shortest paths = BFS layers.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Multi-source BFS starts by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Running separate BFS and taking min without a shared queue
Enqueuing all sources at distance 0 before the loop
questions.explanation: Seed the queue with every source so the first visit is globally nearest.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Implement stack using two queues (costly) typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Makes push O(1) and pop O(1) easily without tricks
Pays O(n) on either push or pop by rotating queues to expose LIFO end
questions.explanation: FIFO structures can simulate LIFO with rotations—some op becomes O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Task scheduler / cooldown problems often combine counting with:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A queue/timeline of when a task becomes available again
questions.explanation: Cooldowns are time-ordered readiness—queue or time buckets help.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Number of islands / rotting oranges grid BFS uses a queue to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Expand infected/visited cells level by level for time or connectivity
Replace DFS entirely in all graph problems forever
questions.explanation: Grid multisource/infection timing is textbook BFS.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A blocking queue in concurrent code provides:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Thread waits on empty take / full put—coordination for producers/consumers
questions.explanation: BlockingQueue couples waiting with capacity—core concurrency primitive.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Backpressure metaphor for a bounded queue means:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Consumers must always outrun producers with infinite buffers
Producers slow/block/drop when consumers lag and the queue fills
questions.explanation: Full buffers push pressure upstream so the system doesn’t OOM.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: First unique character in a stream can use queue + frequency map to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Keep candidate indices in FIFO order and skip those with count > 1
Use only a stack of characters
questions.explanation: Queue holds potential uniques in arrival order; map tracks counts.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why can LinkedList queue suffer more GC/allocator churn than ArrayDeque? Which answer is best?
options: Each element is a separate node object with pointers
ArrayDeque allocates one node per bit
questions.explanation: Per-element nodes cost allocation and pointer chasing.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Level-order zigzag traversal uses a queue (or deque) and:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Requires a priority queue of depths
Alternates direction when emitting each level’s values
Sorts children by value each time
questions.explanation: Still BFS levels; reverse every other level’s output (or use deque ends).

questions.stem: When composing microservices, an unbounded in-memory queue between stages risks:
options: Memory exhaustion under load spikes—prefer bounds + backpressure
questions.explanation: Unbounded queues hide overload until OOM; bounds make failure modes explicit.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Josephus problem variants are often simulated with:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A queue: dequeue and re-enqueue skips until elimination count
questions.explanation: Circular counting maps cleanly to queue rotations.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Hit counter (hits in last 5 minutes) can use a queue of timestamps to:? Which answer is best?
options: Store every hit forever without eviction
Evict timestamps older than the window from the front
questions.explanation: FIFO by time drops expired events in amortized O(1).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: OpenLock / Word Ladder style problems use BFS queues because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Each move has equal cost; first time you reach the target is fewest steps
questions.explanation: Unit-cost state graphs → BFS distances.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A deque-based “sliding window min” differs from max by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Using a queue that is not monotonic
Maintaining increasing candidates at the front instead of decreasing (or vice versa)
questions.explanation: Same monotonic deque idea; inequality direction flips for min vs max.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Senior pitfall: using Stack for BFS “because it’s a list.” What breaks? Which answer is best?
options: You get DFS-ish order; distances/shortest-path arguments for unweighted graphs fail
questions.explanation: BFS correctness depends on FIFO; a stack silently changes the algorithm.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Ring buffer full/empty bug (head==tail meaning both) manifests as:? Choose the strongest option.
options: False full or false empty unless you track size or waste one slot
questions.explanation: Classic fencepost: need a discriminator for the ambiguous state.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Two-stack queue with careless transfer every operation (not lazy) causes:? Which answer is best?
options: O(n) per op and cache-unfriendly churn—lazy pour when out is empty
questions.explanation: Eager full transfers each time destroy the amortization story.

questions.stem: In distributed systems, a local in-process queue is not a substitute for a durable log when:
options: You only need single-threaded in-memory decoupling
Latency must be nanoseconds on one box
You need crash survival and cross-process consumers
questions.explanation: Memory queues die with the process; Kafka/SQS etc. add durability/fan-out.

questions.stem: False sharing / cache-line ping-pong on concurrent ring buffers often comes from:
options: Head and tail counters sharing a cache line between producer and consumer cores
questions.explanation: High-perf queues pad/separate counters; naive layouts thrash caches.

questions.stem: Dropping newest vs oldest on overflow is a product decision. Dropping oldest (coalescing sensors) means:
options: Consumers always see stale spikes only
You keep freshest data, losing history—document the policy
questions.explanation: Overwrite policies trade completeness for freshness; make it explicit.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: BFS memory blow-up on wide graphs: mitigation includes:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Visited/seen marks, bidirectional BFS, or problem-specific pruning
Storing the entire power set of nodes
questions.explanation: Queue width can be huge; visited and bidirectional search reduce frontier size.

questions.stem: Why is “queue via stacks” a common interview question but rare in hot paths?
options: It is asymptotically faster than arrays
Teaching composition of ADTs; arrays/deques win on constants and simplicity
questions.explanation: Interviews test understanding; production picks the straightforward structure.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: At-least-once consumers with a work queue must handle:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Idempotent processing or dedupe because retries redeliver
questions.explanation: Real queues redeliver on failure; consumers must tolerate duplicates.

questions.stem: Using a PriorityQueue when the problem needs stable FIFO among equal priorities leads to:
options: Stable arrival order by default in all libraries
Non-deterministic/unstable ordering among ties unless you add a sequence tie-breaker
questions.explanation: Heaps don’t promise FIFO among equal keys—add insertion index if needed.
